ARTICLE II - DEATH BENEFIT PROVISIONS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2.1 Upon the death of the second insured, we will pay $2,500,000 
    to the designated beneficiary, minus any outstanding loans.

2.2 Proof of both deaths must be submitted:
    • Death certificate of first insured
    • Death certificate of second insured
    • Completed claim forms

2.3 Payment will be made within 30 days of receiving complete 
    documentation.

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARTICLE III - FIRST DEATH BENEFITS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3.1 When the first insured dies, no death benefit is paid, but:
    (a) Policy continues in force
    (b) Premium payment obligation continues
    (c) Policy owner receives written confirmation
    (d) Death certificate must be filed with us

3.2 The surviving insured becomes a single-life whole life policy 
    for administrative purposes only. Coverage amount and premium 
    remain unchanged.

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARTICLE IV - PREMIUM STRUCTURE
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4.1 PREMIUM GUARANTEE PERIOD: Annual premium of $32,400 is 
    guaranteed level for the first 10 policy years.

4.2 AFTER YEAR 10: Premium may increase but will never exceed 
    the maximum guaranteed premium of $48,600 per year.

4.3 Premiums are payable annually on August 20th.

4.4 FIRST DEATH PREMIUM CONTINUATION: Premiums continue after 
    the first death at the same rate.

4.5 WAIVER OPTION: Premium waiver is not available on joint 
    survivorship policies.

4.6 GRACE PERIOD: If payment is not received, you have 60 days 
    to pay before the policy cancels.

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. 21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. 21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

results.question: For claim purposes, if two deaths occur within 30 days of each other, how are they treated?
results.expected_answer: They are treated as simultaneous.
results.evidence: 9.2 If deaths occur within 30 days of each other, this will be treated as simultaneous for claim purposes.
results.llm_answer: For claim purposes, if two deaths occur within 30 days of each other, they are treated as simultaneous deaths, triggering immediate payment of the death benefit.

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

results.question: Why is this policy owned by an irrevocable trust rather than by the insureds directly?
results.expected_answer: The policy is owned by an irrevocable trust to exclude the proceeds from the insureds' taxable estates, as proceeds paid to the trust should not be includable in either insured's estate if the trust is properly structured.
results.evidence: 8.1 This policy is owned by an irrevocable trust to exclude proceeds from the insureds' taxable estates. / 8.2 IMPORTANT TAX NOTE: Proceeds paid to the trust should not be includable in either insured's estate if the trust is properly structured and maintained.
results.llm_answer: The policy is owned by an irrevocable trust to exclude the death benefit from the insureds' taxable estates, supporting estate tax planning and ensuring tax-efficient asset transfer.

1|ESTATE PLANNING LIFE ASSURANCE LTD|Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. is a life insurance company headquartered in Hartford, Connecticut, specializing in the issuance and management of life assurance policies. The company plays a central role in providing financial protection through structured insurance solutions, particularly tailored to estate planning needs. It is responsible for administering policies that include provisions related to ownership, assignment, and free look periods, ensuring policyholders have clear rights and flexibility in managing their coverage. One of its key product offerings is the joint survivorship life insurance policy, which covers two individuals—typically spouses or business partners—and pays the death benefit only upon the death of the second insured. This type of policy is commonly used in estate planning to provide liquidity for estate taxes, business succession, or other financial obligations that arise after the final death of the insured parties. Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. manages these policies throughout their lifecycle, from underwriting and policy issuance to claims processing and benefit disbursement. The company’s operations reflect a focus on long-term financial security, with an emphasis on compliance, customer service, and the precise execution of complex insurance arrangements. Its presence in Hartford, a well-established hub for insurance and financial services, underscores its integration within a robust industry ecosystem. By offering policies with defined ownership structures and clear assignment mechanisms, the company supports clients in achieving their estate planning objectives with confidence and control. Additionally, the inclusion of free look periods allows policyholders to review their coverage within a specified timeframe after purchase, enabling them to cancel or modify the policy if it does not meet their expectations. Overall, Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. serves as a specialized provider of life assurance solutions, particularly for individuals and families seeking to secure their financial legacies through structured, reliable, and professionally managed insurance products.|5

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

1|ESTATE PLANNING LIFE ASSURANCE LTD|Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. is a life insurance company headquartered in Hartford, Connecticut, specializing in the issuance and management of life assurance policies. The company plays a central role in providing financial protection through structured insurance solutions, particularly tailored to estate planning needs. It is responsible for administering policies that include provisions related to ownership, assignment, and free look periods, ensuring policyholders have clear rights and flexibility in managing their coverage. One of its key product offerings is the joint survivorship life insurance policy, which covers two individuals—typically spouses or business partners—and pays the death benefit only upon the death of the second insured. This type of policy is commonly used in estate planning to provide liquidity for estate taxes, business succession, or other financial obligations that arise after the final death of the insured parties. Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. manages these policies throughout their lifecycle, from underwriting and policy issuance to claims processing and benefit disbursement. The company’s operations reflect a focus on long-term financial security, with an emphasis on compliance, customer service, and the precise execution of complex insurance arrangements. Its presence in Hartford, a well-established hub for insurance and financial services, underscores its integration within a robust industry ecosystem. By offering policies with defined ownership structures and clear assignment mechanisms, the company supports clients in achieving their estate planning objectives with confidence and control. Additionally, the inclusion of free look periods allows policyholders to review their coverage within a specified timeframe after purchase, enabling them to cancel or modify the policy if it does not meet their expectations. Overall, Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. serves as a specialized provider of life assurance solutions, particularly for individuals and families seeking to secure their financial legacies through structured, reliable, and professionally managed insurance products.|5

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. 7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

ARTICLE II - DEATH BENEFIT PROVISIONS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2.1 Upon the death of the second insured, we will pay $2,500,000 
    to the designated beneficiary, minus any outstanding loans.

2.2 Proof of both deaths must be submitted:
    • Death certificate of first insured
    • Death certificate of second insured
    • Completed claim forms

2.3 Payment will be made within 30 days of receiving complete 
    documentation.

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARTICLE III - FIRST DEATH BENEFITS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3.1 When the first insured dies, no death benefit is paid, but:
    (a) Policy continues in force
    (b) Premium payment obligation continues
    (c) Policy owner receives written confirmation
    (d) Death certificate must be filed with us

3.2 The surviving insured becomes a single-life whole life policy 
    for administrative purposes only. Coverage amount and premium 
    remain unchanged.

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARTICLE IV - PREMIUM STRUCTURE
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4.1 PREMIUM GUARANTEE PERIOD: Annual premium of $32,400 is 
    guaranteed level for the first 10 policy years.

4.2 AFTER YEAR 10: Premium may increase but will never exceed 
    the maximum guaranteed

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. 7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

1|───────────────────────────
ARTICLE II - DEATH BENEFIT PROVISIONS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2.1 Upon the death of the second insured, we will pay $2,500,000 
    to the designated beneficiary, minus any outstanding loans.

2.2 Proof of both deaths must be submitted:
    • Death certificate of first insured
    • Death certificate of second insured
    • Completed claim forms

2.3 Payment will be made within 30 days of receiving complete 
    documentation.

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARTICLE III - FIRST DEATH BENEFITS
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3.1 When the first insured dies, no death benefit is paid, but:
    (a) Policy continues in force
    (b) Premium payment obligation continues
    (c) Policy owner receives written confirmation
    (d) Death certificate must be filed with us

3.2 The surviving insured becomes a single-life whole life policy 
    for administrative purposes only. Coverage amount and premium 
    remain unchanged.

───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ARTICLE IV - PREMIUM STRUCTURE
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4.1 PREMIUM GUARANTEE PERIOD: Annual premium of $32,400 is 
    guaranteed level for the first 10 policy years.

4.2 AFTER YEAR 10: Premium may increase but will never exceed 
    the maximum guaranteed premium of $48,600 per year.

4.3 Premiums are payable annually on August 20th.

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

7|DEATH BENEFIT|The Death Benefit refers to the $2,500,000 financial payout made to the designated beneficiary upon the death of the second insured, Elizabeth Anne Hartford, as stipulated by the survivorship clause in the life insurance policy. This benefit is triggered exclusively after the death of the second insured, meaning it does not become payable upon the death of the first insured. The policy’s survivorship clause ensures that the death benefit is only released when both insured individuals have passed, with the second death being the determining event. The full $2,500,000 amount is subject to deductions for any outstanding loans against the policy, which may reduce the net payout received by the beneficiary. Additionally, the Death Benefit is governed by standard policy provisions, including clauses related to suicide and contestability, which may affect the eligibility or timing of the payout. For instance, if the death occurs within a specified contestability period (typically two years from the policy’s effective date) and is found to be due to suicide or misrepresentation, the insurer may deny the claim or reduce the benefit accordingly. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. The payout is structured to ensure that the benefit is only distributed after the survivorship requirement is met, reinforcing the policy’s intent to provide support at the conclusion of both insured lives.|9

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

1|ESTATE PLANNING LIFE ASSURANCE LTD|Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. is a life insurance company headquartered in Hartford, Connecticut, specializing in the issuance and management of life assurance policies. The company plays a central role in providing financial protection through structured insurance solutions, particularly tailored to estate planning needs. It is responsible for administering policies that include provisions related to ownership, assignment, and free look periods, ensuring policyholders have clear rights and flexibility in managing their coverage. One of its key product offerings is the joint survivorship life insurance policy, which covers two individuals—typically spouses or business partners—and pays the death benefit only upon the death of the second insured. This type of policy is commonly used in estate planning to provide liquidity for estate taxes, business succession, or other financial obligations that arise after the final death of the insured parties. Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. manages these policies throughout their lifecycle, from underwriting and policy issuance to claims processing and benefit disbursement. The company’s operations reflect a focus on long-term financial security, with an emphasis on compliance, customer service, and the precise execution of complex insurance arrangements. Its presence in Hartford, a well-established hub for insurance and financial services, underscores its integration within a robust industry ecosystem. By offering policies with defined ownership structures and clear assignment mechanisms, the company supports clients in achieving their estate planning objectives with confidence and control. Despite these potential limitations, the Death Benefit remains a significant financial safeguard designed to provide liquidity to the beneficiary upon the final death of the second insured, subject to the policy’s terms, conditions, and any outstanding financial obligations. 1|ESTATE PLANNING LIFE ASSURANCE LTD|Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. is a life insurance company headquartered in Hartford, Connecticut, specializing in the issuance and management of life assurance policies. The company plays a central role in providing financial protection through structured insurance solutions, particularly tailored to estate planning needs. It is responsible for administering policies that include provisions related to ownership, assignment, and free look periods, ensuring policyholders have clear rights and flexibility in managing their coverage. One of its key product offerings is the joint survivorship life insurance policy, which covers two individuals—typically spouses or business partners—and pays the death benefit only upon the death of the second insured. This type of policy is commonly used in estate planning to provide liquidity for estate taxes, business succession, or other financial obligations that arise after the final death of the insured parties. Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. manages these policies throughout their lifecycle, from underwriting and policy issuance to claims processing and benefit disbursement. The company’s operations reflect a focus on long-term financial security, with an emphasis on compliance, customer service, and the precise execution of complex insurance arrangements. Its presence in Hartford, a well-established hub for insurance and financial services, underscores its integration within a robust industry ecosystem. By offering policies with defined ownership structures and clear assignment mechanisms, the company supports clients in achieving their estate planning objectives with confidence and control. Additionally, the inclusion of free look periods allows policyholders to review their coverage within a specified timeframe after purchase, enabling them to cancel or modify the policy if it does not meet their expectations. Overall, Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. serves as a specialized provider of life assurance solutions, particularly for individuals and families seeking to secure their financial legacies through structured, reliable, and professionally managed insurance products.|5

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

9|AUGUST 20, 2024|The date August 20, 2024, marks the official issuance and signing of a joint survivorship life insurance policy by the Chairman & CEO of Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. This date serves as the policy’s issue date, signifying the formal commencement of the policy’s validity. On this day, the document outlining the terms and conditions of the insurance coverage was executed, establishing the foundational agreement between the insurer and the policyholders. As a joint survivorship life insurance policy, the coverage is designed to provide a death benefit payable upon the death of the second insured individual, ensuring financial protection for beneficiaries after both policyholders have passed. The issuance on August 20, 2024, represents the official start of the policy’s effective period, triggering the insurer’s obligations and the policyholders’ rights under the terms specified in the document. This date is critical for determining the policy’s in-force status, premium payment schedules, and the timeline for any potential claims. All policy-related actions, including underwriting approvals, premium due dates, and coverage milestones, are anchored to this commencement date. Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. affirms that the document was duly signed and issued on this date, confirming its legal and operational validity. Thus, August 20, 2024, is both the official start date of the policy’s coverage and the date of formal documentation, ensuring clarity and consistency in the policy’s administration and lifecycle.|4
1|ESTATE PLANNING LIFE ASSURANCE LTD|Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. is a life insurance company headquartered in Hartford, Connecticut, specializing in the issuance and management of life assurance policies. The company plays a central role in providing financial protection through structured insurance solutions, particularly tailored to estate planning needs. It is responsible for administering policies that include provisions related to ownership, assignment, and free look periods, ensuring policyholders have clear rights and flexibility in managing their coverage. One of its key product offerings is the joint survivorship life insurance policy, which covers two individuals—typically spouses or business partners—and pays the death benefit only upon the death of the second insured. This type of policy is commonly used in estate planning to provide liquidity for estate taxes, business succession, or other financial obligations that arise after the final death of the insured parties. Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. manages these policies throughout their lifecycle, from underwriting and policy issuance to claims processing and benefit disbursement. The company’s operations reflect a focus on long-term financial security, with an emphasis on compliance, customer service, and the precise execution of complex insurance arrangements. Its presence in Hartford, a well-established hub for insurance and financial services, underscores its integration within a robust industry ecosystem. By offering policies with defined ownership structures and clear assignment mechanisms, the company supports clients in achieving their estate planning objectives with confidence and control. Additionally, the inclusion of free look periods allows policyholders to review their coverage within a specified timeframe after purchase, enabling them to cancel or modify the policy if it does not meet their expectations. Overall, Estate Planning Life Assurance Ltd. serves as a specialized provider of life assurance solutions, particularly for individuals and families seeking to secure their financial legacies through structured, reliable, and professionally managed insurance products.|5

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4

21|POLICY OWNER|The POLICY OWNER is the individual or entity that holds legal ownership of the life insurance policy, granting them the authority to make key decisions regarding its management and disposition. This includes the right to assign the policy to another party, split it, or otherwise modify its terms in accordance with the policy’s provisions and applicable laws. The POLICY OWNER is responsible for ensuring that premium payments are made on time, as failure to do so may result in the policy lapsing. In addition to financial obligations, the POLICY OWNER is also tasked with handling all administrative actions related to the policy, such as updating beneficiary designations, requesting policy loans, or making changes to coverage. Upon the first death of a covered individual in a joint or survivorship policy, the POLICY OWNER is entitled to receive confirmation of the claim’s processing and the associated benefits, depending on the policy structure. The role of the POLICY OWNER is central to the policy’s lifecycle, as they maintain control over its operation and are the primary point of contact for insurers and financial advisors. This ownership status is distinct from that of the insured or the beneficiary, as the POLICY OWNER holds legal title and decision-making power, even if they are not the individual whose life is insured. The POLICY OWNER may be the same person as the insured, or they may be a separate individual or entity, such as a trust, business, or family member, depending on the policy’s design and the owner’s intentions. In all cases, the POLICY OWNER’s rights and responsibilities are defined by the terms of the insurance contract and are enforceable under the law.|4
